1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). But for
96	\emph{line bundles}, which are flat, that compatibility is \emph{elementary
97	flat-exactness}, not internal-hom machinery. This is exactly the carrier on
98	which the relative Picard group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
99	rests, and it is the subject of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,%
100	sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
101	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
102	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
103	
104	\section{Mathlib API survey}
105	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
106	
107	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
108	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
109	
110	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
111	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
112	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
113	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
114	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
115	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
116	sectionwise tensor product
117	\[
118	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
119	\]
120	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
121	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
122	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
123	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
124	
125	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
126	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
127	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
128	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
129	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
130	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
131	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
132	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
133	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
134	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
135	tensor-product operation
136	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
137	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
138	
139	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
140	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
141	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
142	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
143	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
144	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
145	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
146	\emph{flat-scoped restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
147	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
148	for line bundles \(L, M\) and the restriction along an open immersion
149	\(f\), the canonical comparison
150	\((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes M|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
151	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
152	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume, and for line bundles it is
153	elementary flat-exactness already available in Mathlib (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
154	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
155	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
156	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
157	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
158	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
159	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
160	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
161	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
162	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
163	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
164	independently of any upstream timing.
165	
166	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
167	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
168	
169	\begin{definition}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
173	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
175	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
176	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
177	  \[
178	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
179	  \]
180	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
181	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
182	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
183	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
184	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
185	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
186	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
187	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
188	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
189	  canonical compatibility
190	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
191	     \;\cong\;
192	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
193	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
194	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
195	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
196	
197	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
198	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
199	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
200	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
201	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
202	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
203	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
204	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
205	\end{definition}
206	
207	\begin{lemma}
208	
209	\leanok
210	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
211	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
213	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
214	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
215	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
216	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
217	  \[
218	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
219	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
220	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
221	  \]
222	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
223	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
224	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
225	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
226	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
227	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
228	  \[
229	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
230	    \qquad
231	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
232	  \]
233	  the associator
234	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
235	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
236	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
237	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
238	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
239	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
240	\end{lemma}
241	
242	\begin{proof}
243	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
244	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
245	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
246	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
247	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
248	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
249	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
250	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
251	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
252	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
253	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
254	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{remark}
258	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
259	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
260	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
261	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
262	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
263	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
264	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
265	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
266	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
267	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
268	  depend on it.
269	
270	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
271	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
272	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
273	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
274	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
275	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
276	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
277	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
278	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
279	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
280	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
281	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
282	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
283	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
284	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
285	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
286	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
287	
288	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
289	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the flat-scoped
290	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
291	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
292	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
293	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
294	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
295	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
296	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
297	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
298	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
299	\end{remark}

301	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
302	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
303	
304	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
305	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
306	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
307	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
308	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
309	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the flat-scoped restriction-compatibility
310	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
311	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
312	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
313	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
314	cover, together with their restriction to the
315	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
316	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
317	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
318	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
319	
320	\begin{lemma}
321	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
322	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
323	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
324	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
325	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
326	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
327	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
328	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
329	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
330	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
331	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
332	  \[
333	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
334	      \;\;:=\;\;
335	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
336	  \]
337	  is lax monoidal. This supplies the hypothesis
338	  \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\) required by the
339	  mate construction \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) in the proof of
340	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and is the \emph{sole} genuinely
341	  project-side ingredient that construction depends on.
342	\end{lemma}
343	
344	\begin{proof}
345	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
346	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
347	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
348	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
349	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
350	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
351	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
352	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
353	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
354	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
355	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
356	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
357	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
358	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
359	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
360	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
361	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
362	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
363	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
364	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
365	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
366	
367	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
368	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
369	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
370	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
371	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
372	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
373	\end{proof}
374	
375	\begin{lemma}
376	[Tensor product commutes with restriction for line bundles]
377	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
378	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
380	  % NOTE: the comparison map below is NOT built by hand. It is the
381	  % oplax-monoidal comparison δ of the left adjoint `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ`,
382	  % obtained from the already-present Mathlib construction
383	  % `CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` applied to the present
384	  % adjunction `PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ`. That
385	  % construction's sole hypothesis is `[(pushforward φ).LaxMonoidal]`, and the
386	  % SOLE remaining project-side ingredient is the sectionwise lax-monoidal
387	  % instance of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. This is a bounded
388	  % `mathlib-build` target (one sectionwise lax instance, ~40--90 LOC), not a
389	  % multi-file wall: every other piece (the mate δ, the `comp`, the flat-exactness
390	  % upgrade to an iso) is already in Mathlib.
391	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be line
392	  bundles (equivalently: locally-trivial rank-one modules, hence flat), and
393	  let \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be the restriction along an open immersion.
394	  The canonical comparison morphism
395	  \[
396	    (L \otimes_X M)\big|_f
397	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
398	    L\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; M\big|_f
399	  \]
400	  is an isomorphism. The intended Lean target is
401	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
402	\end{lemma}
403	
404	\begin{proof}
405	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
406	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of (commutative) structure-sheaf-valued
407	  presheaves of rings induced by the open immersion \(f\), so that
408	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor
409	  on presheaves of modules. The proof proceeds by first \emph{constructing}
410	  the comparison morphism as the canonical base-change map of a left adjoint,
411	  and only then upgrading it to an isomorphism using flatness. The earlier
412	  framing --- that flatness alone produces the isomorphism --- is incorrect:
413	  the abstract pullback has no sectionwise formula, so there is no map to
414	  which flatness could be applied until the map has been built.
415	
416	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The
417	  restriction \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion
418	  \(f\) is naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
419	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom}).\mathrm{obj}(-)\),
420	  via the present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\).
421	  It therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism
422	  for the abstract pullback functor.
423	
424	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
425	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
426	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
427	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
428	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
429	   \;\cong\;
430	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
431	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence
432	  the whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the
433	  base-change comparison
434	  \[
435	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
436	      \;\longrightarrow\;
437	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
438	      \;\otimes\;
439	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
440	  \]
441	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\). (This corrects the older
442	  claim that sheafification does not commute with pullback: it does, and the
443	  required natural isomorphism is available.)
444	
445	  \emph{Step 3 (the comparison map is the mate of pushforward).} The functor
446	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is a left adjoint --- it is
447	  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\)
448	  through the present adjunction
449	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi
450	    : \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
451	  The base-change comparison of Step 2 is exactly the oplax-monoidal
452	  comparison \(\delta\) of this left adjoint, obtained by the mate
453	  construction \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\)
454	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Functor}), whose data are
455	  \(\delta_{A,B} = (\mathrm{adj}.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\bigl(
456	    (\eta_A \otimes \eta_B) \circ \mu_{\cdot}\bigr)\) for the unit \(\eta\) of
457	  the adjunction and the lax structure \(\mu\) of the right adjoint. The sole
458	  hypothesis of that construction is that the right adjoint be lax monoidal,
459	  i.e.\ \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\); this is
460	  furnished by \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. The comparison map is
461	  thus produced \emph{abstractly}, with no sectionwise formula required.
462	
463	  \emph{Step 4 (flatness upgrades \(\delta\) to an isomorphism).} It remains
464	  to check that, for line bundles, this already-constructed \(\delta\) is an
465	  isomorphism --- and \emph{this} is where flatness enters. The check is
466	  local, so it suffices to verify it sectionwise, where the comparison is the
467	  ordinary base-change map of modules along a ring map and \(\delta\)
468	  specialises to \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,(-)\) applied to the
469	  structural comparison for the second factor. A line bundle is locally free
470	  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
471	  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
472	  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\).
473	  Tensor is always right-exact, so \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity,
474	  and flatness of \(L\) makes \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve
475	  injectivity (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
476	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
477	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
478	  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
479	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
480	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}). We stress that this step
481	  \emph{upgrades} the comparison \(\delta\) of Step 3 to an isomorphism; it
482	  does \emph{not} construct the map, and is not a substitute for Step 3.
483	
484	  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise/affine isomorphisms of Step 4 are compatible
485	  with the restriction maps, so by \(\mathtt{hom\_ext}\) they assemble into a
486	  single isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules; transporting back through
487	  the isomorphisms of Steps 1--2 yields the asserted global isomorphism
488	  \((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes_U M|_f\).
489	\end{proof}

737	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
738	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
739	
740	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
741	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
742	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
743	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
744	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
745	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
746	
747	\begin{theorem}
748	
749	\leanok
750	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
751	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
752	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
753	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
754	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
755	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
756	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
757	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
758	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
759	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
760	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
761	
762	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
763	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
764	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
765	  % groups)
766	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
767	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
768	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
769	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
770	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
771	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
772	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
773	  % \end{dfn}
774	  %  [\dots]
775	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
776	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
777	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
778	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
779	  % topologies  by
780	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
781	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
782	  %  \end{dfn}"
783	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
784	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
785	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
786	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
787	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
788	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
789	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
790	  \[
791	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
792	  \]
793	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
794	  abelian-group structure with addition
795	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
796	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
797	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
798	  inverse
799	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
800	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
801	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
802	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
803	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
804	  map
805	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
806	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
807	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
808	\end{theorem}
809	
810	\begin{proof}
811	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
812	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
813	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
814	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
815	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
816	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
817	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
818	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
819	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
820	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
821	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
822	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
823	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
824	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
825	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
826	  symmetrically in the second argument. The group axioms are then
827	  \emph{exactly} the four existence-of-isomorphism facts established above,
828	  read on isomorphism classes:
829	  \begin{itemize}
830	    \item \textbf{associativity} \(([L]+[M])+[N] = [L]+([M]+[N])\) is
831	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso};
832	    \item \textbf{neutral element} \([\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}] + [L] = [L]\)
833	      (and on the right) is \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso};
834	    \item \textbf{commutativity} \([L]+[M]=[M]+[L]\) is
835	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso};
836	    \item \textbf{inverse} \([L] + [L^{-1}] = [\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}]\),
837	      with \(L^{-1}=\mathcal{H}om(L,\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T})\), is
838	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
839	  \end{itemize}
840	  Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the existence of an isomorphism ---
841	  so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon, triangle, hexagon) is invoked or
842	  required; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is assembled
843	  directly from these four \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\)
844	  statements. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group:
845	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
846	  (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup})
847	  transported from
848	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\), the
849	  iso-class arithmetic being the family of existence-of-iso facts
850	  \([M \otimes N] = [M]\cdot[N]\) and \([M^{\vee}] = [M]^{-1}\); here the same
851	  shape is transported to the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory
852	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). (The scheme-level Picard
853	  group is genuinely absent from Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}
854	  records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project
855	  supplies it lightly.) The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's
856	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), mirroring Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)
857	  verbatim.
858	
859	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
860	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
861	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
862	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
863	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
864	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
865	
866	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
867	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
868	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
869	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
